name: Psychology
description: A general survey of the major topics in psychology, from biological bases of behavior to mental health and therapy, designed for students with no prior background.

modules.title: What is Psychology?
modules.objectives: Define psychology, trace its history from philosophy to modern science, and identify major psychological perspectives and career paths.
modules.assignment: Interview two adults about what they think psychology studies. Compare their answers to the scientific definition of psychology and write a 250-word reflection on common misconceptions about the field.

modules.title: Research Methods
modules.objectives: Describe the scientific method, distinguish experimental from correlational designs, and explain ethical principles in psychological research.
modules.assignment: Find a news headline reporting a psychological study. Identify the research method used, determine whether the conclusion is causal or correlational, and evaluate two possible alternative explanations for the finding.

modules.title: Biological Bases
modules.objectives: Describe neuron structure and function, identify major brain regions and their roles, and explain how genetics and the nervous system influence behavior.
modules.assignment: Create a labeled diagram of the human brain identifying the major lobes, limbic structures, and brainstem. Write one sentence describing the primary function of each labeled structure.

modules.title: Sensation & Perception
modules.objectives: Distinguish sensation from perception, apply Weber's Law and signal detection theory, and explain key principles of visual and auditory perception.
modules.assignment: Examine three optical illusions and explain each using a principle from perceptual psychology (e.g., Gestalt principles, top-down processing, or size constancy). Provide a sketch or image for each illusion.

modules.title: Learning & Conditioning
modules.objectives: Explain classical and operant conditioning with real examples, compare reinforcement schedules, and describe observational learning.
modules.assignment: Identify one behavior in your own life shaped by classical conditioning and one shaped by operant conditioning. Describe each example using the correct terminology (CS, UCS, CR, reinforcer, etc.) in 250 words total.

modules.title: Memory
modules.objectives: Describe the stages of memory (encoding, storage, retrieval), distinguish types of long-term memory, and explain major causes of forgetting.
modules.assignment: Test yourself on a list of 15 words using free recall immediately after reading, then again 24 hours later. Record your scores and write a 200-word analysis of what happened to your memory over time and why.

modules.title: Thinking & Language
modules.objectives: Describe key concepts in problem solving including heuristics and biases, explain theories of language acquisition, and evaluate the relationship between language and thought.
modules.assignment: Describe a recent decision you made using an example of at least two cognitive biases or heuristics (e.g., availability, anchoring, confirmation bias). Reflect on how these mental shortcuts may have helped or hindered you.

modules.title: Intelligence
modules.objectives: Compare major theories of intelligence (Spearman, Gardner, Sternberg), evaluate IQ testing, and analyze the influence of nature and nurture on intelligence.
modules.assignment: Research and reflect on one alternative theory of intelligence (Gardner, Sternberg, or emotional intelligence). Write a 300-word essay explaining the theory and evaluating whether it captures abilities that standard IQ tests miss.

modules.title: Motivation & Emotion
modules.objectives: Compare theories of motivation, explain Maslow's hierarchy, apply major emotion theories (James-Lange, Cannon-Bard, Schachter-Singer), and evaluate the universality of basic emotions.
modules.assignment: Apply Maslow's hierarchy of needs to your own life. Identify which level you currently spend the most time and energy addressing, provide evidence for your claim, and reflect on how unmet lower-level needs can interfere with higher-level ones.

modules.title: Development
modules.objectives: Summarize key milestones in physical, cognitive, and socioemotional development from infancy through adulthood, and compare Piaget's, Vygotsky's, and Erikson's theories.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Piaget's Stages of Cognitive Development
modules.assignment: Interview a parent or caregiver about a child under the age of 10. Based on the interview, identify which of Piaget's stages the child is in and provide three specific behavioral examples as evidence.

modules.title: Personality
modules.objectives: Compare psychoanalytic, humanistic, and trait theories of personality, evaluate personality assessment tools, and discuss the nature-nurture debate in personality.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Big Five Personality Traits
modules.assignment: Complete a free online Big Five personality assessment. Write a 300-word reflection on your results: which traits seem most accurate, which seem less accurate, and what this suggests about the strengths and limitations of trait-based personality measurement.

modules.title: Social Influences
modules.objectives: Explain conformity, obedience, and the bystander effect; describe the fundamental attribution error; and analyze how social situations shape behavior.
modules.assignment: Describe one personal experience of social pressure (conformity, peer influence, or obedience). Identify the social psychological mechanism at work and evaluate what factors made you more or less likely to comply.

modules.title: Psychological Disorders
modules.objectives: Define psychological disorders using the DSM-5 framework, describe symptoms of anxiety, mood, and psychotic disorders, and evaluate biological vs. psychosocial explanations.
modules.assignment: Choose one psychological disorder from the DSM-5 and research it using at least two credible sources. Write a 350-word case study of a fictional individual, describing the key symptoms, possible causes, and how the disorder affects daily functioning.

modules.title: Therapy & Well-Being
modules.objectives: Compare major therapy approaches (CBT, psychoanalytic, humanistic, biomedical), evaluate their effectiveness, and apply positive psychology principles to personal well-being.
modules.assignment: Design a personal well-being plan using at least three evidence-based strategies from positive psychology (e.g., gratitude journaling, mindfulness, social connection). Implement one strategy for one week and write a 300-word reflection on its effects.

quizQuestions.question: Psychology is best defined as the scientific study of:
options: The brain and nervous system only
quizQuestions.answer: Behavior and mental processes
quizQuestions.explanation: Psychology is the scientific study of behavior (observable actions) and mental processes (thoughts, feelings, and perceptions).

quizQuestions.question: Who is considered the founder of the first psychology laboratory?
quizQuestions.answer: Wilhelm Wundt
quizQuestions.explanation: Wilhelm Wundt established the first experimental psychology laboratory in Leipzig, Germany in 1879.

quizQuestions.question: Which perspective focuses on how thoughts and mental processes influence behavior?
quizQuestions.answer: Cognitive
quizQuestions.explanation: The cognitive perspective studies internal mental processes such as thinking, memory, and decision making.

quizQuestions.question: The behavioral perspective, associated with Watson and Skinner, focuses on:
quizQuestions.answer: Observable behavior shaped by environment
quizQuestions.explanation: Behaviorism focuses exclusively on observable, measurable behavior and how it is shaped by environmental stimuli and reinforcement.

quizQuestions.question: The biopsychosocial model proposes that behavior and health are best understood by examining:
options: Biological, psychological, and social factors together
Genetic factors and early childhood alone
quizQuestions.answer: Biological, psychological, and social factors together
quizQuestions.explanation: The biopsychosocial model integrates biological, psychological, and sociocultural influences on behavior and health.

quizQuestions.question: In a scientific experiment, the independent variable is:
options: The variable that is measured as an outcome
The variable that is manipulated by the researcher
A variable that is held constant across conditions
quizQuestions.answer: The variable that is manipulated by the researcher
quizQuestions.explanation: The independent variable is the factor the experimenter deliberately changes to observe its effect on the dependent variable.

quizQuestions.question: A correlation coefficient of 0.00 indicates:
options: No linear relationship between the variables
quizQuestions.answer: No linear relationship between the variables
quizQuestions.explanation: A correlation of 0 indicates that the two variables are not linearly related — knowing one tells you nothing about the other.

quizQuestions.question: The main advantage of the experimental method over correlational research is that it allows:
options: Study of behavior in natural settings
Use of surveys and self-report measures
quizQuestions.answer: Cause-and-effect conclusions
quizQuestions.explanation: Only true experiments with random assignment allow researchers to draw causal conclusions about the relationship between variables.

quizQuestions.question: Informed consent means that research participants:
options: Can be deceived if the study requires it without their knowledge
Must be told enough to make a voluntary decision about participation
Are required to complete the study once they begin
Need only be informed after the study ends
quizQuestions.answer: Must be told enough to make a voluntary decision about participation
quizQuestions.explanation: Informed consent requires that participants receive adequate information about the study to decide voluntarily whether to participate.

quizQuestions.question: The placebo effect demonstrates that:
options: Expectations alone can cause physiological and psychological changes
Experimental groups always outperform control groups
Research participants always guess the study's purpose
quizQuestions.answer: Expectations alone can cause physiological and psychological changes
quizQuestions.explanation: The placebo effect occurs when participants experience real changes due to their expectations about treatment, not the treatment itself.

quizQuestions.question: Neurons communicate with each other through chemical messengers called:
quizQuestions.answer: Neurotransmitters
quizQuestions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers released from axon terminals that cross the synapse and bind to receptors on the next neuron.

quizQuestions.question: The limbic system is primarily involved in:
quizQuestions.answer: Emotion, memory, and motivation
quizQuestions.explanation: The limbic system, including the hippocampus and amygdala, is critical for emotional processing, memory formation, and motivated behavior.

quizQuestions.question: The 'fight-or-flight' response is controlled by the:
quizQuestions.answer: Sympathetic nervous system
quizQuestions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system activates the body's fight-or-flight response to threats, increasing heart rate, releasing adrenaline, and mobilizing energy.

quizQuestions.question: Neuroplasticity refers to the brain's ability to:
options: Generate unlimited new neurons throughout life
Reorganize and form new connections in response to experience
Resist the effects of brain injury
Maintain a fixed structure from birth onward
quizQuestions.answer: Reorganize and form new connections in response to experience
quizQuestions.explanation: Neuroplasticity is the capacity of the nervous system to change its structure and function in response to learning, injury, or environmental input.

quizQuestions.question: The frontal lobe of the cerebral cortex is most associated with:
quizQuestions.answer: Higher-order thinking, planning, and personality
quizQuestions.explanation: The frontal lobe houses the prefrontal cortex (executive functions) and motor cortex, and is associated with planning, decision making, and personality.

quizQuestions.question: Sensation refers to:
options: Interpreting and making sense of sensory information
The detection and encoding of physical stimuli by sensory receptors
The brain's conscious experience of the world
The filtering of irrelevant sensory information
quizQuestions.answer: The detection and encoding of physical stimuli by sensory receptors
quizQuestions.explanation: Sensation is the process by which sensory receptors detect physical energy and convert it into neural signals.

quizQuestions.question: Sensory adaptation occurs when sensory receptors:
options: Become more sensitive to a stimulus after repeated exposure
Reduce their response to a constant, unchanging stimulus
Begin to detect stimuli they previously could not
Switch from processing one type of stimulus to another
quizQuestions.answer: Reduce their response to a constant, unchanging stimulus
quizQuestions.explanation: Sensory adaptation is the decline in sensitivity to an unchanging stimulus (e.g., no longer noticing the smell of your home after returning).

quizQuestions.question: The retina converts light into neural signals through the action of:
quizQuestions.answer: Cones and rods
quizQuestions.explanation: Rods detect low light (black-and-white), and cones detect color in bright light — together they transduce light into neural signals.

quizQuestions.question: Top-down processing means that perception is driven by:
options: The physical intensity of the stimulus
quizQuestions.answer: Prior knowledge, expectations, and context
quizQuestions.explanation: Top-down processing uses prior knowledge and contextual information to shape how we interpret sensory input.

quizQuestions.question: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus intensity needed to be detected:
quizQuestions.answer: 50% of the time
quizQuestions.explanation: The absolute threshold is defined statistically as the intensity at which a stimulus is detected exactly 50% of the time.

quizQuestions.question: In classical conditioning, the unconditioned response (UCR) is:
options: A learned response to the CS
A natural, unlearned reaction to the UCS
The pairing of the neutral stimulus with the UCS
quizQuestions.answer: A natural, unlearned reaction to the UCS
quizQuestions.explanation: The UCR is the unlearned, automatic response naturally triggered by the unconditioned stimulus before any conditioning occurs.

quizQuestions.question: Positive reinforcement _____ a behavior by _____ a stimulus.
quizQuestions.answer: Increases; adding
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive reinforcement increases the likelihood of a behavior by adding a pleasant consequence after the behavior occurs.

quizQuestions.question: Stimulus generalization in classical conditioning means:
options: The CR no longer occurs after extinction
The CR transfers to stimuli similar to the original CS
The CR occurs only to the exact original CS
The UCS becomes unnecessary after conditioning
quizQuestions.answer: The CR transfers to stimuli similar to the original CS
quizQuestions.explanation: Generalization is the tendency to produce a conditioned response to stimuli that resemble the original conditioned stimulus.

quizQuestions.question: Observational learning (Bandura) requires which key mental process?
options: Physical rehearsal of all observed actions
quizQuestions.answer: Attention, retention, reproduction, and motivation
quizQuestions.explanation: Bandura's model of observational learning requires attention to the model, retention of the behavior, ability to reproduce it, and motivation to do so.

quizQuestions.question: A child who touches a hot stove and gets burned is less likely to touch it again. This is an example of:
quizQuestions.answer: Positive punishment
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive punishment decreases behavior by adding an aversive stimulus (pain from the burn) after the behavior (touching the stove).

quizQuestions.question: Short-term memory typically holds approximately how many items?
quizQuestions.explanation: George Miller's research showed that short-term memory holds approximately 7 (plus or minus 2) chunks of information.

quizQuestions.question: Procedural memory stores:
quizQuestions.answer: Motor skills and habits
quizQuestions.explanation: Procedural memory is a type of implicit long-term memory that stores skilled actions and habits (e.g., how to ride a bike).

quizQuestions.question: According to the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve, most forgetting occurs:
quizQuestions.answer: Shortly after learning
quizQuestions.explanation: Ebbinghaus showed that forgetting is steepest immediately after learning and then slows, forming the iconic forgetting curve.

quizQuestions.question: Retrograde amnesia involves:
options: Loss of memories for events before brain injury
quizQuestions.answer: Loss of memories for events before brain injury
quizQuestions.explanation: Retrograde amnesia is the loss of memories that were formed before the onset of brain damage or injury.

quizQuestions.question: The encoding specificity principle states that retrieval is best when:
options: The retrieval context matches the encoding context
quizQuestions.answer: The retrieval context matches the encoding context
quizQuestions.explanation: Encoding specificity: cues present at encoding help retrieval — being in the same environment or mood as during learning improves recall.

quizQuestions.question: A heuristic is best described as:
options: A mental shortcut that allows quick but sometimes inaccurate judgments
quizQuestions.answer: A mental shortcut that allows quick but sometimes inaccurate judgments
quizQuestions.explanation: Heuristics are efficient cognitive shortcuts that guide rapid judgments but can lead to systematic errors.

quizQuestions.question: The availability heuristic involves judging the probability of an event based on:
options: How easily examples come to mind
quizQuestions.answer: How easily examples come to mind
quizQuestions.explanation: The availability heuristic uses mental ease of retrieval as a proxy for frequency, which can cause overestimation of dramatic or memorable events.

quizQuestions.question: According to Chomsky, the ability to understand and produce language is:
options: Partly innate — humans have a biological capacity for language
Determined solely by the language environment
quizQuestions.answer: Partly innate — humans have a biological capacity for language
quizQuestions.explanation: Chomsky argued that humans are biologically predisposed to learn language through an innate language acquisition device.

quizQuestions.question: Confirmation bias refers to the tendency to:
options: Seek information that confirms existing beliefs
Change beliefs when confronted with contradictory evidence
Use only logical reasoning in decision making
Rely on statistics rather than examples
quizQuestions.answer: Seek information that confirms existing beliefs
quizQuestions.explanation: Confirmation bias causes people to favor, seek, and remember information that aligns with their preexisting beliefs.

quizQuestions.question: Critical period in language development refers to:
options: The age when children begin formal schooling
A sensitive developmental window when language learning is most effective
The period when vocabulary growth is fastest
The time when a child first becomes bilingual
quizQuestions.answer: A sensitive developmental window when language learning is most effective
quizQuestions.explanation: There is a critical (or sensitive) period in early childhood during which language acquisition proceeds most naturally and completely.

quizQuestions.question: Spearman's 'g' factor refers to:
options: A general factor underlying performance across diverse cognitive tasks
quizQuestions.answer: A general factor underlying performance across diverse cognitive tasks
quizQuestions.explanation: Spearman used factor analysis to identify g — a general intelligence factor that contributes to performance on all cognitive tasks.

quizQuestions.question: IQ tests are considered valid if they:
options: Produce the same score every time
Accurately predict academic performance and other real-world outcomes
Are taken by a large number of people
Contain a wide variety of question types
quizQuestions.answer: Accurately predict academic performance and other real-world outcomes
quizQuestions.explanation: Validity means the test measures what it claims to measure — IQ tests are validated against criteria like academic achievement.

quizQuestions.question: Fluid intelligence involves:
options: Reasoning and problem solving in novel situations
quizQuestions.answer: Reasoning and problem solving in novel situations
quizQuestions.explanation: Fluid intelligence is the ability to think flexibly and solve new problems, independent of accumulated knowledge.

quizQuestions.question: Howard Gardner proposed that intelligence:
quizQuestions.answer: Consists of multiple distinct domains
quizQuestions.explanation: Gardner's theory of multiple intelligences identifies distinct abilities (e.g., linguistic, musical, spatial, interpersonal) that IQ tests fail to capture.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of stereotype threat (Steele & Aronson) shows that awareness of a negative stereotype:
options: Can impair performance on relevant tasks
Only affects those with low self-esteem
quizQuestions.answer: Can impair performance on relevant tasks
quizQuestions.explanation: Stereotype threat is the risk of confirming a negative group stereotype, which creates anxiety that disrupts cognitive performance on relevant tasks.

quizQuestions.question: Drive-reduction theory proposes that motivation arises from:
options: The desire to reach one's full potential
Biological needs that create tension (drives) which behavior aims to reduce
quizQuestions.answer: Biological needs that create tension (drives) which behavior aims to reduce
quizQuestions.explanation: Drive-reduction theory holds that biological imbalances create drives (e.g., hunger) that motivate behavior aimed at restoring equilibrium.

quizQuestions.question: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs:
options: At the top of the pyramid
At the second level, after safety
At the base of the pyramid
In the middle of the pyramid
quizQuestions.answer: At the base of the pyramid
quizQuestions.explanation: Physiological needs (food, water, shelter, warmth) are the most basic and form the foundation of Maslow's hierarchy.

quizQuestions.question: The Schachter-Singer two-factor theory of emotion proposes that emotional experience depends on:
options: Physiological arousal plus a cognitive label for that arousal
quizQuestions.answer: Physiological arousal plus a cognitive label for that arousal
quizQuestions.explanation: Schachter-Singer theory: emotions arise from undifferentiated physiological arousal that we then label based on contextual cues.

quizQuestions.question: Intrinsic motivation involves engaging in an activity:
options: To earn a grade or payment
Because the activity itself is inherently rewarding
quizQuestions.answer: Because the activity itself is inherently rewarding
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation is self-generated — the person engages because the activity is enjoyable or personally meaningful.

quizQuestions.question: Paul Ekman's research on emotions found that basic facial expressions:
quizQuestions.answer: Are universally recognized across cultures
quizQuestions.explanation: Ekman's cross-cultural research found that expressions of six basic emotions (happiness, sadness, fear, disgust, anger, surprise) are recognized universally.

quizQuestions.question: Object permanence develops during which of Piaget's stages?
quizQuestions.answer: Sensorimotor
quizQuestions.explanation: Object permanence — the understanding that objects continue to exist when out of sight — develops during the sensorimotor stage (birth to age 2).

quizQuestions.question: Egocentrism in Piaget's preoperational stage means:
options: Selfishness and lack of caring for others
Inability to take others' visual or cognitive perspectives
Focus on one's own moral development
quizQuestions.answer: Inability to take others' visual or cognitive perspectives
quizQuestions.explanation: Egocentrism is the cognitive limitation of being unable to view the world from another person's perspective (not a moral flaw).

quizQuestions.question: Erikson's stage of 'Intimacy vs. Isolation' occurs during:
quizQuestions.answer: Early adulthood
quizQuestions.explanation: In early adulthood, individuals focus on forming close, committed relationships or risk feeling isolated and alone.

quizQuestions.question: Longitudinal studies in developmental psychology:
options: Study different age groups at one point in time
Follow the same group of individuals over an extended period
Use experimental manipulation to study development
quizQuestions.answer: Follow the same group of individuals over an extended period
quizQuestions.explanation: Longitudinal studies track the same participants over time, allowing direct observation of developmental change.

quizQuestions.question: Puberty is primarily triggered by changes in the:
quizQuestions.answer: Endocrine (hormonal) system
quizQuestions.explanation: Puberty is initiated by the release of sex hormones from the endocrine system, triggering physical and sexual maturation.

quizQuestions.question: Freud's id operates according to the:
quizQuestions.answer: Pleasure principle
quizQuestions.explanation: The id is the primitive part of the personality that seeks immediate gratification of desires without regard for reality or morality.

quizQuestions.question: The Big Five personality trait of 'conscientiousness' refers to:
quizQuestions.answer: Dependability, organization, and self-discipline
quizQuestions.explanation: Conscientiousness captures individual differences in organization, diligence, and goal-directed self-discipline.

quizQuestions.question: Carl Rogers believed psychological health requires:
options: Congruence between one's self-concept and actual experience
Achievement of the highest level of Maslow's hierarchy
Successful completion of all psychosocial stages
quizQuestions.answer: Congruence between one's self-concept and actual experience
quizQuestions.explanation: Rogers proposed that psychological well-being requires alignment between who we think we are (self-concept) and our actual experience.

quizQuestions.question: The Rorschach inkblot test is a _____ assessment tool.
quizQuestions.answer: Projective personality
quizQuestions.explanation: The Rorschach test presents ambiguous inkblots and asks participants to describe what they see, assuming responses reveal unconscious content.

quizQuestions.question: Bandura's concept of self-efficacy refers to:
options: The belief in one's ability to succeed at specific tasks
The tendency to observe and imitate others
A stable personality trait of confidence
quizQuestions.answer: The belief in one's ability to succeed at specific tasks
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-efficacy is the task-specific belief in one's capability to execute the behaviors required to achieve a goal.

quizQuestions.question: The fundamental attribution error is the tendency to:
options: Attribute our own failures to bad luck
Over-attribute others' behavior to their personality and underweight situational factors
Blame situations for our own behavior and others for theirs
quizQuestions.answer: Over-attribute others' behavior to their personality and underweight situational factors
quizQuestions.explanation: The FAE leads us to view others' behavior as reflecting their character, ignoring the situational pressures they face.

quizQuestions.question: In Milgram's experiments, about _____ of participants delivered what they believed was the maximum shock.
quizQuestions.explanation: Approximately 65% of participants followed orders to the maximum level, demonstrating the power of authority.

quizQuestions.question: The bystander effect refers to:
options: Greater helping when more people are present
Reduced helping in emergencies when more people are present
Conformity to group norms about helping
quizQuestions.answer: Reduced helping in emergencies when more people are present
quizQuestions.explanation: The bystander effect: as the number of observers increases, each individual feels less personally responsible to help.

quizQuestions.question: In-group bias refers to the tendency to:
options: Favor one's own group members over members of other groups
Be critical of one's own group's behavior
Help out-group members more than in-group members
quizQuestions.answer: Favor one's own group members over members of other groups
quizQuestions.explanation: In-group bias involves evaluating and treating members of one's own group more positively than out-group members.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive dissonance theory predicts that people will change their _____ to match their _____.
quizQuestions.answer: Attitudes; behavior (or vice versa)
quizQuestions.explanation: Cognitive dissonance theory predicts that people modify attitudes or behaviors to reduce the inconsistency between them.

quizQuestions.question: According to the DSM-5, a psychological disorder involves:
options: Any behavior considered unusual by the general public
Clinically significant disturbance in thought, emotion, or behavior causing distress or impairment
A behavior that occurs less than once per month
quizQuestions.answer: Clinically significant disturbance in thought, emotion, or behavior causing distress or impairment
quizQuestions.explanation: The DSM-5 defines mental disorders as clinically significant disturbances that cause distress or impair functioning.

quizQuestions.question: Major depressive disorder requires symptoms lasting at least:
quizQuestions.answer: 2 weeks
quizQuestions.explanation: DSM-5 criteria for MDD require five or more symptoms most of the day, nearly every day, for at least 2 weeks.

quizQuestions.question: A hallucination is:
options: A false belief that is firmly maintained despite contradictory evidence
A sensory experience that occurs without an external stimulus
An intense, irrational fear of a specific object
quizQuestions.answer: A sensory experience that occurs without an external stimulus
quizQuestions.explanation: Hallucinations are perceptions (e.g., hearing voices, seeing things) in the absence of external stimuli.

quizQuestions.question: Generalized Anxiety Disorder is characterized by:
options: Specific fears triggered by identifiable objects or situations
Excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple everyday concerns
Intrusive thoughts followed by compulsive rituals
quizQuestions.answer: Excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple everyday concerns
quizQuestions.explanation: GAD involves pervasive anxiety and worry about a wide range of topics that the person finds difficult to control, lasting at least 6 months.

quizQuestions.question: The diathesis-stress model proposes that psychological disorders result from:
options: An interaction between biological vulnerability and environmental stressors
quizQuestions.answer: An interaction between biological vulnerability and environmental stressors
quizQuestions.explanation: The diathesis-stress model holds that disorders emerge when a genetic or biological predisposition is triggered by environmental stressors.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) primarily targets:
quizQuestions.answer: Maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors
quizQuestions.explanation: CBT targets the distorted beliefs and dysfunctional behaviors that maintain psychological disorders.

quizQuestions.question: Antidepressants in the SSRI class work by:
options: Preventing serotonin reuptake, increasing its availability
quizQuestions.answer: Preventing serotonin reuptake, increasing its availability
quizQuestions.explanation: SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) block the reabsorption of serotonin into presynaptic neurons, increasing serotonin levels in the synapse.

quizQuestions.question: Positive psychology focuses on:
options: Studying and building human strengths and well-being
quizQuestions.answer: Studying and building human strengths and well-being
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive psychology (Seligman, Csikszentmihalyi) focuses on what makes life worth living — strengths, virtues, and flourishing.

quizQuestions.question: The therapeutic technique of systematic desensitization is used primarily to treat:
quizQuestions.answer: Phobias and anxiety disorders
quizQuestions.explanation: Systematic desensitization pairs relaxation with gradual exposure to feared stimuli, extinguishing the conditioned fear response.

quizQuestions.question: Mindfulness-based therapies focus on:
options: Non-judgmental awareness of the present moment
quizQuestions.answer: Non-judgmental awareness of the present moment
quizQuestions.explanation: Mindfulness practices cultivate present-moment, non-judgmental awareness, which has been shown to reduce stress, anxiety, and depression.

questions.question: Psychology is defined as the scientific study of:
questions.answer: Behavior and mental processes
questions.explanation: Psychology studies observable behavior and internal mental processes using scientific methods.

questions.question: The independent variable in an experiment is:
options: The factor manipulated by the researcher
questions.answer: The factor manipulated by the researcher
questions.explanation: The independent variable is deliberately changed by the experimenter to observe its effect on the dependent variable.

questions.question: Neurotransmitters are released from the:
questions.answer: Axon terminals
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are stored in vesicles at the axon terminals and released into the synapse upon stimulation.

questions.question: Transduction is the process by which sensory receptors:
options: Interpret sensory information using prior knowledge
Convert physical energy into neural signals
questions.answer: Convert physical energy into neural signals
questions.explanation: Transduction is the conversion of physical stimuli (light, sound, pressure) into neural impulses the brain can process.

questions.question: In classical conditioning, before training the neutral stimulus:
questions.answer: Produces no relevant response
questions.explanation: Before conditioning, the neutral stimulus does not trigger any response; only after pairing with the UCS does it become a CS.

questions.question: Which memory system has the smallest capacity and shortest duration?
questions.answer: Sensory memory
questions.explanation: Sensory memory holds large amounts of sensory information very briefly (less than 1 second for iconic memory) before most of it fades.

questions.question: The availability heuristic leads to errors because:
options: We judge frequency by how easily examples come to mind, not actual statistics
We anchor to an initial estimate
questions.answer: We judge frequency by how easily examples come to mind, not actual statistics
questions.explanation: Vivid or recent events are more easily recalled and thus overestimated in frequency.

questions.question: Gardner's theory of multiple intelligences proposes that intelligence:
options: Peaks in early adulthood and declines
questions.answer: Consists of multiple distinct capacities
questions.explanation: Gardner identified at least eight intelligences (linguistic, logical, musical, etc.) as distinct abilities rather than a single g factor.

questions.question: The Schachter-Singer two-factor theory proposes that emotions consist of:
options: Physiological arousal followed by cognitive labeling
questions.answer: Physiological arousal followed by cognitive labeling
questions.explanation: Schachter and Singer proposed that we experience undifferentiated arousal and then label it as a specific emotion based on contextual cues.

questions.question: Piaget's sensorimotor stage ends with the emergence of:
questions.answer: Object permanence
questions.explanation: Object permanence — understanding that objects continue to exist when out of sight — is a key achievement of the sensorimotor stage.

questions.question: Positive punishment decreases behavior by:
questions.answer: Adding an aversive stimulus
questions.explanation: Positive punishment adds an unpleasant consequence after a behavior, reducing its future frequency.

questions.question: The sympathetic nervous system prepares the body for:
questions.answer: Fight or flight response
questions.explanation: The sympathetic branch of the autonomic nervous system activates the body's fight-or-flight response to perceived threats.

questions.question: Intrinsic motivation involves engaging in a task:
options: Because the activity is inherently enjoyable or interesting
questions.answer: Because the activity is inherently enjoyable or interesting
questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from within — the drive to engage with a task for its own inherent satisfaction.

questions.question: Sensory adaptation explains why you stop noticing:
options: The smell of your own home after returning from a trip
questions.answer: The smell of your own home after returning from a trip
questions.explanation: Sensory adaptation causes receptors to reduce their response to constant, unchanging stimuli — we stop consciously perceiving them.

questions.question: The egocentrism of Piaget's preoperational stage means:
options: Children are selfishly focused on their own needs
Children cannot take another person's perspective
Children prefer their own company over others
Children believe they are always correct
questions.answer: Children cannot take another person's cognitive or visual perspective
questions.explanation: Egocentrism is a cognitive limitation — children in the preoperational stage cannot mentally represent how the world looks from another's point of view.

questions.question: The Big Five personality trait of neuroticism refers to:
options: Emotional instability and tendency toward negative emotions
questions.answer: Emotional instability and tendency toward negative emotions
questions.explanation: High neuroticism is characterized by anxiety, moodiness, and emotional reactivity.

questions.question: The fundamental attribution error involves:
options: Over-weighting situational causes for others' behavior
Over-weighting dispositional causes for others' behavior
Attributing our own behavior to personality
Seeing our own actions as atypical
questions.answer: Over-weighting dispositional causes for others' behavior
questions.explanation: The FAE is the widespread tendency to explain others' behavior in terms of character (disposition) while ignoring situational pressures.

questions.question: Generalized Anxiety Disorder is defined by worry that:
options: Focuses on one specific feared object
Is excessive, uncontrollable, and affects multiple life domains
questions.answer: Is excessive, uncontrollable, and affects multiple life domains
questions.explanation: GAD is characterized by pervasive, uncontrollable anxiety and worry about many aspects of life, lasting at least 6 months.

questions.question: Cognitive-behavioral therapy focuses on changing:
questions.answer: Maladaptive thoughts and behaviors
questions.explanation: CBT targets dysfunctional thinking patterns and behaviors that maintain psychological distress.

questions.question: The diathesis-stress model proposes that disorders result from:
options: An interaction between biological vulnerability and stress
questions.answer: An interaction between biological vulnerability and stress
questions.explanation: Disorders develop when a biological predisposition (diathesis) interacts with significant environmental stressors.

questions.question: Self-actualization, the highest need in Maslow's hierarchy, refers to:
questions.answer: Realizing one's full human potential
questions.explanation: Self-actualization is Maslow's term for the drive to fulfill one's unique capabilities and live a fully authentic, meaningful life.

questions.question: Bystander effect is primarily caused by:
options: Diffusion of responsibility among multiple observers
Lack of empathy in modern society
Fear of personal injury when helping
questions.answer: Diffusion of responsibility among multiple observers
questions.explanation: When multiple people witness an emergency, each individual assumes others will take responsibility, reducing any one person's likelihood of helping.

questions.question: Erikson's stage of 'Generativity vs. Stagnation' occurs in:
questions.answer: Middle adulthood
questions.explanation: In middle adulthood, the central challenge is generativity — contributing to society through work, parenting, or community — vs. stagnation.

questions.question: Freud's defense mechanism of rationalization involves:
options: Creating logical-sounding justifications for unacceptable feelings or actions
Returning to childlike behavior under stress
Channeling impulses into socially acceptable activities
questions.answer: Creating logical-sounding justifications for unacceptable feelings or actions
questions.explanation: Rationalization is a defense mechanism in which plausible but false reasons are constructed to justify unacceptable behavior or feelings.

questions.question: SSRIs are primarily used to treat:
questions.answer: Depression and anxiety disorders
questions.explanation: SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) are first-line pharmacological treatments for major depression and anxiety disorders.

questions.question: Variable-ratio reinforcement schedules produce behavior that is:
options: High in rate and highly resistant to extinction
Fast but easily extinguished after reinforcement stops
questions.answer: High in rate and highly resistant to extinction
questions.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules produce high, steady response rates and extreme resistance to extinction because each response may bring reinforcement.

questions.question: Procedural memory is considered implicit because:
options: It requires conscious effort to retrieve
It operates without conscious awareness and is difficult to verbalize
It can only be acquired in childhood
questions.answer: It operates without conscious awareness and is difficult to verbalize
questions.explanation: Procedural (skill) memory is implicit — we perform learned skills automatically without consciously accessing the stored information.

questions.question: Hallucinations are most characteristic of which disorder?
questions.answer: Schizophrenia
questions.explanation: Hallucinations (especially auditory hallucinations, e.g., hearing voices) are among the most prominent positive symptoms of schizophrenia.

questions.question: Positive psychology is best described as the scientific study of:
options: Optimistic thinking patterns in cognitive therapy
questions.answer: Human strengths, virtues, and flourishing
questions.explanation: Positive psychology studies what makes individuals and communities thrive — focusing on strengths rather than deficits.

questions.question: The 'just noticeable difference' (JND) in psychophysics refers to:
options: The minimum stimulus detectable 50% of the time
The smallest change in stimulus intensity detectable
The maximum stimulus intensity a person can endure
questions.answer: The smallest change in stimulus intensity detectable
questions.explanation: The JND (difference threshold) is the minimum difference between two stimuli that can be reliably detected.

questions.question: The concept of fluid intelligence refers to:
options: Abstract reasoning and novel problem solving
questions.answer: Abstract reasoning and novel problem solving
questions.explanation: Fluid intelligence is the capacity to reason flexibly and solve new problems, independent of previously acquired knowledge.

questions.question: Systematic desensitization treats phobias by pairing:
options: Relaxation with gradual exposure to the feared stimulus
The feared stimulus with a neutral stimulus
Imagery of the feared stimulus with intense arousal
questions.answer: Relaxation with gradual exposure to the feared stimulus
questions.explanation: Systematic desensitization extinguishes conditioned fear by gradually pairing anxiety-provoking stimuli with a relaxation response.

questions.question: Groupthink is a form of:
options: Informational social influence causing accurate group decisions
Faulty group decision making driven by desire for consensus
questions.answer: Faulty group decision making driven by desire for consensus
questions.explanation: Groupthink involves the suppression of critical thinking to maintain group harmony, leading to poor collective decisions.

questions.question: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with Alzheimer's disease?
questions.answer: Acetylcholine
questions.explanation: Alzheimer's disease involves the degeneration of acetylcholine-producing neurons, significantly impairing memory and cognitive function.

questions.question: Client-centered therapy (Rogers) is characterized by:
options: Unconditional positive regard, empathy, and genuineness
questions.answer: Unconditional positive regard, empathy, and genuineness
questions.explanation: Rogers' client-centered approach creates a therapeutic environment of unconditional positive regard, empathic understanding, and therapist genuineness to promote growth.
